message:
feat(core): support table-aliased star expansion in GROUP BY (bd-2wiu)

Handle SELECT tt.* FROM t AS tt GROUP BY ... by checking both the
table name and alias when expanding TableStar columns.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-4x6i","title":"frankentui Dashboard: Guided corruption -> repair storyline view","description":"## Goal
Add a guided demo mode in the dashboard that runs the durability story and displays steps as they happen.

## Steps UI
- encode sidecar
- corrupt file
- sqlite fails (show error)
- repair progress
- final integrity + sha match

## Acceptance
- One command runs the full storyline with on-screen status.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:23:24.272829223Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:53.276321691Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:53.276307545Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of existing E2E suite tasks; superseded by canonical bd-z0xm subgraph.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["durability","e2e","frankentui","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-4x6i","depends_on_id":"bd-1szk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:23:34.265332856Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1145,"issue_id":"bd-4x6i","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"bd-4xpo","title":"Enable e2e logical state hash assertions now that ORDER BY rowid works","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:38:27.439302847Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:49:11.015360868Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T17:49:11.015341853Z","close_reason":"Enabled logical state hash assertions now that ORDER BY rowid works; correctness_sequential_insert + correctness_mixed_dml assert hash.matched; tests pass","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-5ic3","title":"Metadata/manifest hardening: stable JSON schema + backwards-compatible versioning","description":"## Goal

Ensure `metadata/*.json` and `manifests/manifest.v1.json` are stable contracts we can build automation/UI on.

## Requirements

- JSON schema (`manifest.v1.schema.json`) stays in sync with actual manifest.
- Deterministic ordering:
  - entries sorted by db_id
  - table lists and PRAGMA lists sorted
- Versioning:
  - explicit `manifest_version`
  - ability to introduce v2 without breaking tooling

## Acceptance

- A test enforces: schema validates manifest, and manifest+checksums+metadata are mutually consistent.
- Any future field additions are additive and do not break old readers.

(We still do not care about external backwards compatibility; this is about our own tooling stability.)","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance Criteria

1. Manifest schema stability:
   - `sample_sqlite_db_files/manifests/manifest.v1.schema.json` validates `manifest.v1.json` in tests.
   - Manifest includes an explicit `manifest_version` (v1) and is designed so v2 can be introduced without breaking v1 tooling.

2. Deterministic generation:
   - Manifest entries are sorted by `db_id`.
   - Nested lists (tables, pragma summaries) use a deterministic order.

3. Cross-artifact consistency is enforced:
   - Every manifest entry has a corresponding `metadata/<db_id>.json` and checksum entry.
   - Every checksum entry refers to an existing golden file.

4. Regression tests:
   - CI test fails with a clear message if schema/manifest mismatch or ordering drift occurs.","notes":"Notes

- This is about *our internal tooling contract*, not external backwards compatibility.
- Prefer additive evolution; avoid renaming/removing fields unless the manifest version is bumped.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:25:35.061448742Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:16:32.453890968Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T19:16:32.453869939Z","close_reason":"Added CI test validation that manifest.v1.json conforms to manifest.v1.schema.json (Draft 2020-12) using jsonschema; existing tests already enforce ordering and manifest/checksums/metadata consistency.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corpus","e2e"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-5ic3","depends_on_id":"bd-1n0i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:26:06.418178816Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-5ic3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:46:57.537666448Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-6d9v","title":"Benchmark: large transaction (100K/1M row single-txn insert)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:19:20.878949954Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T06:44:38.288627054Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T06:44:38.288608479Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-6d9v","depends_on_id":"bd-1pdl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:19:31.262595986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1050,"issue_id":"bd-6d9v","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation matches the task title: Benchmark: large transaction (100K/1M row single-txn insert) 
- All unit tests pass; code compiles without warnings under clippy pedantic+nursery
- Integration with existing e2e harness framework verified
- No unsafe code, no panics in normal operation
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:09Z"},{"id":1067,"issue_id":"bd-6d9v","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Benchmark — large transaction (100K/1M row single-txn insert)

### What
Measure performance of very large transactions that stress the WAL and page cache.

### Benchmark Design
- Single transaction: BEGIN, INSERT N rows, COMMIT
- N = 100,000 and 1,000,000
- Table with 5 columns: id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT, score REAL, created TEXT
- Row size ~200 bytes → 100K rows ≈ 20MB, 1M rows ≈ 200MB

### Why This Matters
Large transactions exercise:
- WAL growth (C SQLite: WAL file grows until checkpoint)
- MVCC version chain (FrankenSQLite: page versions accumulate)
- Memory pressure (page cache turnover)
- Commit latency (fsync of large WAL vs MVCC commit)

### Metrics
- Total time for INSERT phase
- Commit latency (time from last INSERT to COMMIT completion)
- Peak memory usage during transaction
- WAL file size at commit time (C SQLite) / version chain depth (FrankenSQLite)
- Post-VACUUM database size (should be identical)

### Expected Behavior
- FrankenSQLite may have advantages in commit speed (no WAL checkpoint needed)
- C SQLite may have advantages in insert speed (mature, optimized C code)
- Memory characteristics will differ due to MVCC vs WAL approach

### Acceptance Criteria
- Benchmarks for both 100K and 1M row transactions
- Total time, commit latency, and memory reported
- Both backends produce same final state (logical comparison)
- No OOM or crash on 1M row transaction
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:43Z"}]}
